title: Cron Expression Tester (preview next 5 runs)
shortDescription: Test cron expressions and see the next 5 scheduled runs in your local timezone. Validates fields, supports ranges, lists, and steps.
description: Type a 5-field cron expression (min, hour, day-of-month, month, day-of-week) and see the next 5 trigger times. Common presets included.
metaDescription: Free cron expression tester. Validate syntax and preview the next 5 trigger times in your local timezone. Standard 5-field cron with ranges, lists, and steps.

sections.heading: Cron syntax in five fields
paragraphs: Standard cron has 5 space-separated fields: minute (0-59), hour (0-23), day of month (1-31), month (1-12), day of week (0-6, 0=Sunday). Each field accepts: a single value (5), a range (1-5), a list (1,3,5), a step (*/15 means every 15), or wildcard (*). Combinations work: 1-5,10/2 means 1-5 then every 2 starting from 10.

This tester uses the strict POSIX/Linux crontab dialect. Some systems extend cron with named months (JAN-DEC) or weekdays (SUN-SAT) — those aren't supported here. Use numeric values for full compatibility.

sections.heading: Common patterns
paragraphs: */5 * * * * — every 5 minutes. Useful for cache refreshes and short polls.
0 0 1 * * — midnight on the 1st of every month. Monthly billing or stats rollups.
*/15 9-17 * * 1-5 — every 15 minutes during business hours, weekdays. Active-hour polling.

sections.heading: Day-of-month vs day-of-week
paragraphs: Both fields accept values, but watch out: in the standard dialect, when both fields are set (not '*'), the cron runs when EITHER condition matches, not both. So `0 0 1 * MON` (1st of month, OR Monday) fires more often than you might think.

To restrict to 'first Monday of the month', cron alone can't do it directly — you need to either run a script daily and check day-of-month <= 7 inside, or use a scheduler (Airflow, GitHub Actions schedule + check) that supports more expressive scheduling.

howTo.name: Type a cron expression
howTo.text: 5 fields: minute, hour, day-of-month, month, day-of-week.

howTo.name: Read next 5 runs
howTo.text: Times in your local browser timezone. Useful to verify a schedule before deploying.

faq.q: Why do I see 5 runs in my local timezone?
faq.a: Cron is always in the host's local timezone. Your browser's timezone is what's displayed here. Real cron daemons honor the server's TZ; check your host.
Not yet — only numeric values. We may add JAN/MON-style aliases later.
In standard cron they're identical. Some Quartz dialects use ? to distinguish 'unspecified' for the day-of-month vs day-of-week fields. Standard cron treats them the same.

faq.q: Why doesn't day-of-month + day-of-week work like I expect?
faq.a: When both fields aren't '*', standard cron uses OR (matches either). To get AND ('first Monday of month'), you need a wrapper script or a more expressive scheduler.
Standard cron is minute-precision. Some systems add a sixth field for seconds; we don't support that. If you need second precision, use setInterval or a different scheduler.
Minute-level. Schedules finer than 1 minute aren't expressible in cron.

faq.q: Can I test cron expressions for years in the past?
faq.a: We compute forward from 'now'. For historical analysis, use a programming language's cron library.

faq.q: Does the data leave my browser?
faq.a: No. Parsing and computation are all local.
